title: Justice
html:
Big Question

What do I owe? And to whom?

Jericho was a prosperous city, a crossroads for trade caravans, and Zacchaeus was the chief tax collector. Publicans, as they were called, were despised twice over: they collaborated with the Roman Empire occupying Israel and they enriched themselves by overcharging their own countrymen. Zacchaeus wasn't just any collector; he was the architelones, the chief publican of Jericho. Rich, hated, and — literally — short in stature.

When Jesus passed through the city, Zacchaeus wanted to see him but the crowd blocked his way. So he did something unthinkable for a man of his position: he ran and climbed a sycamore tree. A wealthy chief climbing a tree like a child. Jesus saw him, called him by name, and invited himself to stay at his house. The crowd murmured: «He has gone to be the guest of a sinner» (Lk 19:7).

But what happened inside that house changed everything. Zacchaeus, standing up — that physical detail matters because Zacchaeus was short; standing up was an act of recovered dignity — said: «Lord, I will give half of my possessions to the poor, and if I have defrauded anyone, I will repay four times as much» (Lk 19:8). It wasn't Jesus who asked for that. It was Zacchaeus who offered it. The encounter with Christ awakened in him an awareness of justice.

Jesus responded: «Today salvation has come to this house» (Lk 19:9). Note: salvation did not come when Zacchaeus believed intellectually. It came when Zacchaeus gave back. Justice was the door to salvation. Because justice is not an optional decoration of the Christian life; it is the skeleton of charity. Without justice, love is sentimentality. With justice, love becomes flesh, becomes a check, becomes restitution.

This lesson is about justice: the virtue that gives each person their due. But «their due» isn't just my money or my things. It is also my dignity, my work, my just wage, my participation in the community. Justice, the Catechism teaches, is the virtue that sustains all social life. And as Zacchaeus discovered, there is no true encounter with Christ that does not lead to an act of justice.

Activity

Think: if Jesus invited himself to your house today, as he did with Zacchaeus, what would you have to give back? Not just money: perhaps a pending apology, a poorly done job, the credit you stole from a colleague, the time you owe your family. Zacchaeus put numbers on it: half to the poor, four times what was defrauded. What numbers would you put?

1. The virtue of justice: giving each their due

The Catechism places justice among the cardinal virtues — those four virtues that are like the hinges (cardo in Latin) of the moral life: prudence, justice, fortitude, and temperance. The classic definition comes from Roman law and was taken up by St. Thomas Aquinas: justice is the moral virtue that consists in the constant and firm will to give God and neighbor what is owed to them (CCC 1807).

The detail is crucial: «constant and firm will.» Justice does not depend on mood. The person who pays when in a good mood and haggles when angry is not just. Justice is a habit, a stable disposition of character. That is why it is a virtue: not an isolated act, but a way of being.

The three types of justice

The Thomistic tradition, incorporated by the Catechism, distinguishes three forms of justice according to who owes what to whom (CCC 1877-1889, 2411):

1. Commutative justice — regulates relations between persons and between private individuals. It is the justice of exchange: what I owe you, what you owe me. It includes respect for contracts, payment of debts, restitution of what was stolen, a just wage for work performed. When an employer pays less than agreed, he violates commutative justice. When an employee deliberately works poorly, she does too. It is symmetrical: both parties have reciprocal rights and obligations.

2. Legal justice — regulates what the citizen owes to the community. It is not «legal» in the sense of «what positive law says,» but in the sense of «what the natural law demands for the common good.» The payment of just taxes, service to the community, obedience to legitimate laws, contribution to social order. St. Paul expressed it this way: «Pay to all what is due them: taxes to whom taxes are due, revenue to whom revenue is due, respect to whom respect is due, honor to whom honor is due» (Rom 13:7).

3. Distributive justice — regulates what the community owes to each citizen. It is the justice of distribution: how society distributes burdens and benefits among its members. Access to education, healthcare, housing, social protection. It is asymmetrical: public authority has the obligation to distribute equitably, attending to each person's needs and their contribution to the common good. It is not «the same to everyone» — equity is not egalitarianism — but «to each according to their need, their merit, and their function.»

These three forms of justice are complementary. A society cannot function if citizens steal from one another (lack of commutative justice), if no one contributes to the common good (lack of legal justice), or if the authority distributes resources arbitrarily or in a partisan way (lack of distributive justice). All three are necessary and none substitutes for the others.

Context

In the ancient world, justice was represented as a woman with scales and a blindfold. The scales mean that she weighs actions and gives each their due. The blindfold means she does not look at whom she judges: she does not care if you are rich or poor, known or unknown. Justice is blind to persons but clear-sighted to facts. This image, coming from the Greek goddess Themis, was Christianized by medieval tradition and today adorns courthouses around the world. But there is a detail the ancient world did not know: for the Christian, justice is not just an abstract ideal. It is a Person. Jesus said: «Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they will be filled» (Mt 5:6).

Christian justice is not cold. It hungers. It thirsts. It is passionate. Because it does not seek only the balance of the scales; it seeks the restoration of the person.

2. The seventh commandment: «You shall not steal»

The seventh commandment of the Decalogue — «You shall not steal» (Ex 20:15) — seems one of the most obvious. But the Catechism devotes considerable space to it (CCC 2401-2463) because its scope is much broader than it first appears. Not stealing means respecting others' goods, but it also means ordering the economy to the service of the human person, respecting the integrity of creation, paying just wages, not engaging in usury, not defrauding the tax authorities, not corrupting public officials.

Behind the seventh commandment lies a profound doctrine on property and the destination of goods. The Catechism teaches two principles that must be held in tension (CCC 2402-2406):

First: the universal destination of goods. «God destined the earth and all it contains for the use of all men and all peoples. Consequently, created goods should flow fairly to all, under the guidance of justice and accompanied by charity» (CCC 2402, quoting Vatican II, GS 69). This means that the earth belongs to no one in an absolute sense. Its ultimate owner is God, and he has given it to all humanity. No person, no company, no country can say: «This is mine and I do with it whatever I want.» Property carries a social mortgage.

Second: the right to private property. This right is legitimate and necessary. Private property guarantees freedom, the autonomy of persons and families, the fruit of one's own labor. But this right is not absolute. We are stewards, not absolute owners (CCC 2403). The parable of the talents (Mt 25:14-30) teaches precisely this: the goods we have have been entrusted to us to make them bear fruit, not to bury them or squander them. And one day we will render an account.

St. John Paul II summarized it in his encyclical Centesimus Annus (1991): «God gave the earth to the whole human race for the sustenance of all its members, without excluding or favoring anyone.» Private property is a means, not an end. The end is that all human beings may live with dignity.

«Let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream.»

— Amos 5:24

3. The social doctrine of the Church: fundamental principles

The social doctrine of the Church (SDC) is neither a political ideology nor a party platform. It is the application of the Gospel to social, economic, and political life. The Catechism summarizes its basic principles in paragraphs 1883-1890, and the Compendium of the Social Doctrine of the Church (2004) develops them systematically. There are four pillars:

The dignity of the human person

This is the foundation of everything. The person is an end in himself, never a means. Society exists for the person, not the person for society. This means that no economic structure, no public policy, no company can treat human beings as interchangeable parts. The person has inalienable rights derived from his nature created in the image of God, not from the concession of the State.

The common good

Vatican II gave the classic definition that the Catechism incorporates: the common good is «the sum total of social conditions which allow people, either as groups or as individuals, to reach their fulfillment more fully and more easily» (GS 26, cited in CCC 1906). It is not the sum of individual goods, nor is it the good of the State above its citizens. It is that fabric of conditions — peace, justice, freedom, education, healthcare, work — that allows each person to achieve their fullness. The common good requires three elements (CCC 1907-1909): respect for the person, social development, and peace and security.

Subsidiarity

Classically formulated by Pius XI in Quadragesimo Anno (1931), the principle of subsidiarity establishes that problems should be resolved at the level closest to the citizen. The State should not intervene in what families, local communities, and intermediate associations can do for themselves. And conversely: the State should intervene to support and supply what lower levels cannot achieve. «A community of a higher order should not interfere in the internal life of a community of a lower order, depriving the latter of its functions, but rather should support it in case of need» (CCC 1883).

Solidarity

It is not a vague feeling of compassion. St. John Paul II defined it as «a firm and persevering determination to commit oneself to the common good; that is to say, to the good of all and of each individual» (Sollicitudo Rei Socialis, 38). Solidarity is the virtue that recognizes that we are all responsible for all. There is no justice without solidarity, because justice alone is not enough: that bond of fraternity that goes beyond what is strictly owed is also needed.

Connection

What do these principles have to do with your daily life? Think about your family. A family functions when each member is respected in their dignity (no favoritism or mistreatment), the good of all is sought (not just of the strongest), decisions are made at the appropriate level (parents don't control every detail of their teenagers' lives, but they are present), and solidarity exists (when one is suffering, everyone rallies). Society is nothing more than a family on a larger scale. What works in the family — justice and love — is what should work in society. John Paul II called this analogy «the civilization of love.»

4. The economy at the service of the human person

The Catechism states clearly: «Man is the author, the center, and the end of all economic and social life» (GS 63, cited in CCC 1892). The economy is not a neutral force; it has a moral dimension. Not everything that is technically efficient is morally acceptable. The market is a useful instrument, but it is not a god. And when the market becomes an idol to which human lives are sacrificed, it becomes corrupt.

The dignity of work

Work is not a punishment. It is a participation in God's creative work. From Genesis, where God places man in the garden «to till it and keep it» (Gen 2:15), work is part of the human vocation. The Church's tradition on work is extremely rich:

  • Leo XIII, in Rerum Novarum (1891), the first great social encyclical, defended the right of workers to a just wage, to rest, and to form unions. At the height of the Industrial Revolution, when workers were treated as disposable parts, the Church raised its voice.
  • Pius XI, in Quadragesimo Anno (1931), deepened the teaching on social justice and subsidiarity.
  • John Paul II, in Laborem Exercens (1981), declared that «work is for man, not man for work.» The worker is always more important than the product he makes. The human being is not a tool.

The Church teaches that access to work is a right. And work must be dignified: with a just wage that allows the worker and his family to live decently, with safe conditions, with the right to rest, and with the possibility of fulfilling religious and family duties (CCC 2434). Denying these conditions is an injustice that cries out to heaven.

Economic sins

The Catechism lists with precision the violations of the seventh commandment in the economic sphere (CCC 2407-2418):

  • Theft and larceny: taking what belongs to another against their will.
  • Fraud: deceiving in contracts, weights, measures, or product quality.
  • Usury: lending money demanding abusive interest rates that exploit another's need.
  • Unjust wages: paying less than what is necessary for a dignified life. The Catechism classifies this as one of the sins that «cry out to heaven» (CCC 1867).
  • Corruption: bribing officials, using public power for private gain.
  • Tax evasion: not paying just taxes. It is not a «national sport»; it is an offense against legal justice that deprives the community of resources for the common good.
  • Harm to creation: the irresponsible destruction of the environment is also a sin against justice, because it deprives future generations of the resources that belong to them (CCC 2415).

St. John Chrysostom, a Doctor of the Church of the fourth century, said it provocatively: «Not sharing our goods with the poor is robbing them and taking their life. The goods we possess are not ours, but theirs.» It is a strong statement, but it points to the truth: when we accumulate far more than we need while others lack the essentials, something is deeply broken in our sense of justice (CCC 2446).

Going Deeper

The Thomistic distinction between justice and charity illuminates this entire topic. For St. Thomas (STh II-II, q.58, a.1-12), justice regulates what is owed; charity, what is given freely. Justice says: «I owe you this, and I pay it.» Charity says: «I don't owe you anything, but I give it to you freely, because I love you.» Both are necessary. A society without justice is a jungle. A society without charity is an efficient but cold machine, incapable of forgiveness, incapable of gratuitousness.

John Paul II, in his encyclical Dives in Misericordia (1980), shows that justice alone is not enough to build a humane society. The person who only asks for justice receives what is theirs — but the one who receives mercy receives something they did not deserve and that can transform them. Zacchaeus's experience is exactly that: Jesus does not demand justice from him; he gives him mercy, and that mercy awakens in Zacchaeus the desire to do justice. Mercy does not cancel justice; it makes it fruitful.

Recommended reading: Compendium of the Social Doctrine of the Church, chapters 3 and 4. Also YouCat 321-328 on property and social justice.

5. The preferential option for the poor

The most shattering Gospel passage about justice is the final judgment according to St. Matthew. Christ, the King and Judge, separates the nations and says to those on his right: «Come, you that are blessed by my Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world; for I was hungry and you gave me food, I was thirsty and you gave me drink, I was a stranger and you welcomed me, I was naked and you clothed me, I was sick and you visited me, I was in prison and you came to me» (Mt 25:34-36). And then he reveals the mystery: «Truly I tell you, just as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me» (Mt 25:40).

The preferential option for the poor, formulated by Latin American theology and enshrined by the Magisterium of John Paul II and Benedict XVI, does not mean that God loves the poor more than the rich. It means that God's justice leans toward those the world despises and that the judgment on our Christian life passes through how we have treated those who can return nothing to us (CCC 2443-2449). As Pope Francis said: «The poor are the flesh of Christ.»

The works of mercy: justice in action

Christian tradition distinguishes fourteen works of mercy, seven corporal and seven spiritual, which are the practical translation of justice and charity. The corporal works (CCC 2447): feeding the hungry, giving drink to the thirsty, clothing the naked, visiting the imprisoned, sheltering the homeless, visiting the sick, burying the dead. The spiritual works: instructing the ignorant, counseling the doubtful, admonishing the sinner, forgiving offenses, comforting the afflicted, bearing wrongs patiently, praying for the living and the dead.

These works are not optional. At the final judgment, we will not be asked whether we went to Mass every Sunday — we will be asked whether we fed the hungry. Faith without works is a corpse, says James (Jas 2:17). Justice without mercy is cold calculation. But mercy without justice to make it concrete is empty feeling.

«What good is it, my brothers and sisters, if someone says he has faith but does not have works? Can that faith save him? If a brother or sister is naked and lacks daily food, and one of you says to them, “Go in peace; keep warm and eat your fill,” and yet you do not supply their bodily needs, what is the good of that? So faith by itself, if it has no works, is dead.»

— James 2:14-17

6. Restitution and peace

The Catechism is unequivocal: the absolution of sins does not cancel the obligation to repair the harm caused. «The harm done must be repaired (restitution). Forgiveness does not remove the need for reparation. Justice requires the restitution of what was stolen» (CCC 2412). Zacchaeus understood it before the Catechism existed: it was not enough to repent. He had to give back, and give back four times over.

Restitution is not a footnote to morality; it is the very bone of commutative justice. If you stole, give it back. If you slandered, retract. If you damaged someone's reputation, restore it as far as possible. If you exploited your workers, compensate them. There is no valid confession without a purpose of amendment, and amendment includes, whenever possible, the reparation of harm. Canon law reflects this and moral theology has always taught it.

And from restitution peace is born. The prophet Isaiah coined the formula that Christian tradition has never ceased to repeat:

«The effect of righteousness will be peace, and the result of righteousness, quietness and trust forever.»

— Isaiah 32:17

Pius XII adopted the motto opus iustitiae pax (peace is the work of justice) as the axis of his pontificate amid the Second World War. There is no peace without justice. Wars, social tensions, bloody revolutions are not born from nothing: they are born from accumulated injustices that were never repaired. Justice is not a luxury for calm times. It is the foundation upon which peace is built (CCC 2304).

Our Lady of Guadalupe, patroness of the Americas and Empress of Mexico, is a perfect icon of this truth. When the Morenita appeared to St. Juan Diego on Tepeyac Hill in December 1531, Mexico's indigenous peoples were living under the trauma of the Conquest: decimated, humiliated, dispossessed. Mary did not appear to the Spanish bishop. She appeared to an Indian. She spoke to him in Nahuatl. She called him «the least of my sons.» She said to him: «Am I not here, I who am your Mother?» In that gesture, the Mother of God restored the dignity of a people and laid the foundations of the justice that Mexico still seeks. Guadalupe is the symbol of a justice that does not humiliate but lifts up; that does not punish but restores.

7. Witnesses of justice: Romero, Dorothy Day, and the Cristeros

St. Óscar Romero (1917-1980), Archbishop of San Salvador, was a shy, scholarly, conservative man. His fellow bishops chose him for the see of San Salvador precisely because he wouldn't cause trouble. But three weeks after his appointment, his friend Fr. Rutilio Grande was murdered by death squads for defending peasants. Romero went to the town where Rutilio had been killed, saw the corpses, saw the fear in the eyes of the poor... and something broke inside him. He could no longer keep silent. His Sunday homilies, broadcast to the whole country, denounced disappearances, torture, starvation wages. «The Church cannot keep silent in the face of injustice,» he would say. He was assassinated while celebrating Mass, on March 24, 1980, with a single bullet to the chest. His conversion to justice cost him his life. Canonized by Pope Francis in 2018, he is today the saint who reminds us that following Christ seriously means defending the poor to the very end.

Dorothy Day (1897-1980) was a journalist, socialist, bohemian, and atheist before her conversion to Catholicism. When she was baptized, she did not abandon her passion for social justice; she deepened it. She founded the Catholic Worker movement: houses of hospitality for the homeless, free soup kitchens, newspapers sold for a penny. She lived among the poor, shared her life with them, and was imprisoned several times for protesting against war and nuclear weapons. Her cause for canonization is open. «The Church must remember that the Gospel is preached to the poor,» Dorothy wrote, «and the poor are the ones who understand it best.»

The Cristiada (1926-1929) was the war Mexican Catholics waged against the religious persecution of Plutarco Elías Calles' government. But it was not just a war for religious freedom. For the Cristeros, faith was not something private kept in the closet; it was the soul of their identity, the basis of their justice, the foundation of their dignity as a people. Entire families of peasants, many without formal education, understood that denying God in the public square was accepting a radical injustice against the human being. They died crying «¡Viva Cristo Rey!» Today, many of them are canonized or beatified — St. José Sánchez del Río, just 14 years old, tortured and executed, refused to renounce his faith and is the best known of these witnesses. The Cristiada teaches that justice sometimes demands defending the truth in public life, not just in the temple.

Context

Chesterton said that «the problem is not that men don't believe in God; the problem is that they stop believing in justice.» And he added: «When a man stops believing in God, it isn't that he believes in nothing. It is that he believes in anything.» Substituting God with the State, with the market, with ideology, or with oneself produces profoundly unjust societies, because only God guarantees the absolute dignity of every person. Without him, the strong always find an excuse to crush the weak.

In What's Wrong with the World (1910), Chesterton wrote: «The Catholic Social Doctrine is the only creed that really believes in freedom, because it is the only one that believes that every human soul is worth more than the entire universe.»

For Reflection

1. Zacchaeus gave back four times what he had stolen. What would you need to give back for your life to be just? Think of concrete things: money, time, reputation, attention, effort, forgiveness.

Justice is not abstract. It has numbers, names, and dates. If you can't put numbers on your debt, you're probably not taking it seriously.

2. Do you believe you pay the people who work for you (employees, suppliers, services) a fair price? Or do you always look for the bargain even though you know the other person loses?

Unjust wages are one of the sins that cry out to heaven (CCC 1867). You don't need to be a big businessman: you can also exploit the person who helps you at home, someone doing an occasional job for you, or the unpaid intern.

3. St. John Chrysostom said that not sharing with the poor is robbing them. Does that seem exaggerated to you? Why? What does this statement say about the way you live?

Look at your spending from the last month. How much was for you, how much for others, and how much for the poor? You don't need to tell us the answer. Just give it to yourself.

4. The principles of the Church's social doctrine — dignity of the person, common good, subsidiarity, solidarity — are they present in your way of thinking about politics and economics? Or do you let your political ideology determine your morality instead of the other way around?

Being Catholic in public life does not mean joining a political party. It means judging all political proposals from the principles of the Gospel. That will make you uncomfortable for every party — and free in relation to all of them.

5. Have you ever suffered a grave injustice? Have you been able to forgive it? Have you fought to repair it without hatred? Peace is the work of justice (opus iustitiae pax). How can you be a builder of peace in a concrete conflict in your life?

Peace is not the absence of fighting. Peace is the restoration of what was broken. Sometimes it is restored by giving back; sometimes, by forgiving what can no longer be returned.

Key Takeaway

Justice is not a political opinion. It is a cardinal virtue of the Christian. Giving each their due — to God, to neighbor, to the community — is the structure on which charity is built. Without justice, love is empty talk. With justice, love is Zacchaeus climbing down from the sycamore tree and opening his wallet.

Cultural Resources

Film Romero (John Duigan, 1989), with Raúl Juliá as St. Óscar Romero. The film follows the transformation of a shy bishop into a prophet of justice. The final scene, during Mass, is overwhelming. Also The Mission (Roland Joffé, 1986) about the Jesuit reductions in South America and the conflict between justice and colonial power.

Mexican Cinema For Greater Glory (Dean Wright, 2012), starring Andy García and Eva Longoria, tells the epic of the Cristeros. It sends shivers down your spine. The Great Silence about St. José Sánchez del Río is also a gem. Also Guadalupe: Mother of Humanity (2023).

Literature G.K. Chesterton, What's Wrong with the World (1910). A classic that never ages. Chesterton defends the family, distributed property (distributism), freedom, and human dignity with devastating humor. C.S. Lewis, Mere Christianity, chapter on social morality. Dorothy Day, The Long Loneliness, autobiography. And the Compendium of the Social Doctrine of the Church (2004).

Social Encyclicals The complete sequence: Rerum Novarum (Leo XIII, 1891), Quadragesimo Anno (Pius XI, 1931), Mater et Magistra (John XXIII, 1961), Pacem in Terris (John XXIII, 1963), Populorum Progressio (Paul VI, 1967), Laborem Exercens (John Paul II, 1981), Sollicitudo Rei Socialis (John Paul II, 1987), Centesimus Annus (John Paul II, 1991), Caritas in Veritate (Benedict XVI, 2009), Laudato Si' (Francis, 2015). All available free at vatican.va.

Mexico Our Lady of Guadalupe appeared to a humble Indian at the moment of greatest historical injustice against his people. Her image on Juan Diego's tilma is the best treatise on justice Mexico has ever received: the Mother of God becomes mestiza and says to the last ones: «Am I not here, I who am your Mother? What troubles you?» Read the Nican Mopohua, the original account in Nahuatl. And visit the Basilica if you can. It is not tourism: it is justice.

Art The Disasters of War by Goya (1810-1820), a series of engravings documenting the horrors of the Spanish War of Independence. Without an explicit moral, they question the viewer: «Is this what we do?» Also The Haywain by Bosch, an allegory of human greed that drags everyone to hell.

Music Solo le pido a Dios by León Gieco («I only ask God that war not be indifferent to me»). The Misa Criolla by Ariel Ramírez. In English, Blowin' in the Wind by Bob Dylan, and Where Is the Love? by The Black Eyed Peas. Hakuna Group Music, especially «Justicia» and «Revolución.» And the WYD Lisbon 2023 anthem, «Há pressa no ar» (Mary arises in haste), about the urgency of justice.

Audiovisual Catechesis Real + True (osvnews.com) has episodes on social justice. Fr. Mike Schmitz, on Ascension Presents, has excellent videos on the Social Doctrine. In Spanish, the channel Misioneros Digitales Católicos and Padre Luis Toro. Also YouCat 321-334 on justice and property.
